text:
Congo: Colonel Mobutu reportedly has agreed to the formation of a permanent government and the reopening of parliament on 10 January 1961, provided that most members of parliament are able to reach Leopoldville by then and any proposed government is acceptable to himself and Kasavubu. He remains contemptuous of Kasavubu's supporters, however, and might still try to install a government of his own choosing.

Meanwhile, Ghanaian President Nkrumah appears to be attempting to persuade Nasir to leave the UAR's troops in the Congo. Nkrumah apparently has secured Nasir's agreement to begin the formation of an African military command outside the UN framework, and two Egyptian officers are to be sent to Ghana for this purpose

text:
III. THE WEST

Ecuador: Anti-US sentiment is increasing in Ecuador and pressure is building up for the establishment of relations with the USSR and Communist China as a result of the 7 December declaration by the guarantor powers of the Rio Protocol. The guarantors are the United States, Brazil, Chile, and Argentina. The declaration upheld the validity of the Rio Protocol of 1942, which provides for a definitive settlement of the border dispute between Ecuador and Peru, and denied the right of either country to renounce the protocol unilaterally.
